data.title: STERIS (STE) Stock Could Be A Bargain On Its 16% Slide
data.description:
STERIS stock has fallen 15.8% year to date, yet the current Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) intrinsic value estimate still points to meaningful upside relative to the share price, creating a tension between the weak share performance and what the valuation models suggest.

  • Year to date, STERIS is down 15.8%, which means recent shareholders are sitting on losses even though the latest intrinsic value work indicates potential upside.
  • Expectations that STERIS can continue to generate reliable cash flows may support the intrinsic value estimate, while any pressure on margins or a sustained slowdown in cash generation would be a clear risk to that view.
  • Simpler checks show STERIS only passes 2 out of 6 valuation tests. This leans more toward the stock not being a clear bargain on the broader metrics despite the supportive DCF signal, according to this valuation summary.

The issue now is whether STERIS deserves to trade closer to its intrinsic value estimate, or whether the low broader value score and recent share price weakness are warning that the current discount is justified.

STERIS delivered -6.3% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Medical Equipment industry.

Advertisement

Is STERIS a Bargain on Cash Flow?

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) approach values STERIS by projecting its future cash generation and discounting it back to today. On this model, STERIS is producing latest twelve month free cash flow of about $946.7 million, with the projections assuming broadly growing cash flows rather than a sharp step change.

Those cash flows translate into an estimated intrinsic value of about $249 per share, compared with a market price that implies the stock trades at roughly a 15.4% discount to that estimate. The result is a picture where STERIS screens as undervalued on cash flow, even though it does not look inexpensive on simpler multiples such as P/E or P/S.

Overall, the Discounted Cash Flow analysis indicates that STERIS stock appears undervalued relative to its modeled cash flows.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests STERIS is undervalued by 15.4%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 38 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for STERIS.

Where Does STERIS Sit on Earnings?

The P/E ratio is a useful cross-check for STERIS because it anchors the share price to the earnings the business is currently producing. STERIS trades on a P/E of about 26.2x, which is close to the Medical Equipment industry average of roughly 26.4x and modestly above the peer group average of about 21.3x. On raw market comparisons, the stock does not screen obviously cheap, but it also does not sit at an extreme premium to its sector.

The fair P/E ratio estimate for STERIS is around 24.8x, which reflects what investors might reasonably pay given its size, margins and risk profile. That is only slightly below the current multiple, so the gap is relatively small and does not suggest a strong discount or an aggressive premium. Taken together with the industry and peer checks, STERIS appears broadly aligned with where similar stocks trade on earnings.

Putting the P/E work together, STERIS stock appears roughly fairly valued on its earnings multiple.

See what the numbers say about this price — find out in our valuation breakdown.

The STERIS Narrative: What Would Justify Today's Price?

Simply Wall St Narratives aim to connect STERIS' current valuation puzzle with clear, quantified views of what its future growth, margins and earnings would need to look like for the stock to be worth materially more or materially less than today's price. Each narrative links its number to a specific view on how STERIS' growth, profitability and risks might evolve, giving you something concrete to revisit on the Community page as new information comes through.

If you have a clear, number-driven view on where STERIS' growth, margins and execution go from here, share a Narrative and put your case on the record for others to weigh against the current valuation.

It is a chance to add your voice to the Simply Wall St community, spell out the assumptions behind your outlook, and track how your thesis holds up as new results come through.

Do you think there's more to the story for STERIS? Head over to our Community to see what others are saying!

The Bottom Line

For STERIS, the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) work points to intrinsic value above the current share price, while the market multiple view suggests the stock is priced roughly in line with peers. That gap reflects a classic tension between what its cash generation could be worth over time and what investors are currently prepared to pay based on earnings comparisons. With broader valuation checks scoring weakly, the key question is whether STERIS can sustain the cash flows implied in the intrinsic value estimate or whether the current discount is simply the market pricing in the risk that margins and cash generation come under pressure.

data.title: 3 AI Infrastructure Stocks With Revenue Growth Retail Investors Should Watch
data.description:
AI infrastructure stocks sit at the crossroads of powerful themes investors are watching right now, from shifting inflation patterns and resilient services activity in Europe and the UK, to changing trade flows across Asia and emerging markets. While software headlines grab attention, the physical backbone of AI, including data centers, power, and connectivity, is where demand translates into actual cash flow. This article focuses on an AI Infrastructure Stocks screener built to highlight companies that already show revenue growth and solid operational efficiency, and presents three stocks from that list for investors to study more closely.

Advertisement

Lumentum Holdings (LITE)

Overview: Lumentum Holdings is a photonics manufacturer that supplies lasers, optical chips, modules, and subsystems used in cloud data centers, AI and machine learning infrastructure, and high speed telecom networks, as well as industrial lasers for semiconductor, solar, display, and electric vehicle manufacturing. The company sells these hardware components globally to large cloud operators, network equipment makers, and advanced manufacturing customers.

Operations: Lumentum generates revenue across multiple regions, with US$523.1m from the United States, US$494.4m from Thailand, US$460.7m from Hong Kong, US$318m from Mexico, US$256.1m from Other Asia Pacific, US$178.7m from Europe, the Middle East and Africa, and smaller contributions from Japan and Other Americas, plus a segment adjustment of US$142.4m.

Market Cap: US$64.8b

Lumentum is positioned in AI data center optics, where demand for high bandwidth connections and co packaged optics has attracted the attention of major brokers and led to strong earnings expectations, including forecasts for earnings and revenue growth that outpace the wider US market and Communications sector. At the same time, the stock carries a high P/E multiple, a balance sheet funded entirely by external borrowings, management with a relatively short tenure, and recent insider selling. These factors raise questions about how durable the current optimism is. For investors who want exposure to AI infrastructure, Lumentum presents a combination of potential growth drivers alongside financial and governance risks that may warrant closer inspection before any decision is made.

Surging interest in AI optics has Lumentum at the center of the conversation, but the headline story may not match the full risk reward profile. Before you decide how it fits your portfolio, walk through the 4 key rewards and 2 important warning signs

Jabil (JBL)

Overview: Jabil is a global manufacturing and engineering company that designs and builds electronics, hardware platforms, and complex systems for customers in areas like AI data centers, 5G, healthcare, automotive, and consumer devices, while also managing supply chains and digital commerce solutions. It works behind the scenes for many large brands, providing everything from product design and prototyping to full scale production, testing, and direct fulfillment.

Operations: Jabil generates most of its revenue from its Intelligent Infrastructure segment at US$15.8b, followed by Regulated Industries at US$12.4b and Connected Living and Digital Commerce at US$5.4b.

Market Cap: US$33.4b

Jabil gives investors direct exposure to AI infrastructure build out, with its Intelligent Infrastructure segment supplying hardware and systems to hyperscale data centers, while new projects in India and the US expand that footprint. Recent guidance points to AI driven revenue and earnings expectations, supported by forecast returns on equity and active share buybacks. This comes with a thin net margin of about 2.6% and a balance sheet that relies heavily on external funding. Weakness in EV and renewable energy customers, tariff uncertainty, and newer board oversight add further questions. For investors weighing AI hardware beneficiaries, the key consideration is how this growth narrative compares with those financial and governance risks.

Jabil’s AI fueled revenue story and share buybacks are grabbing attention, but the real question is how the growth case stacks up against those slim margins and funding risks. Tap into the analyst forecasts for Jabil for the missing context investors are debating.

Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT)

Overview: Vertiv Holdings Co builds and services the power, cooling, and rack systems that keep data centers and other mission critical facilities running, including those that support AI, cloud computing, and online services. Its hardware, software, and lifecycle services are used by large technology and industrial customers across the Americas, Asia Pacific, Europe, the Middle East, and Africa.

Operations: Vertiv generates most of its revenue in the Americas at US$7.0b, with US$2.4b from Asia Pacific and US$2.3b from Europe, the Middle East and Africa, partly offset by US$973.8m of intersegment sales.

Market Cap: US$115.7b

Vertiv sits at the center of AI’s physical build out. It supplies liquid cooling and power systems that data centers need as GPU racks consume ever more energy. Its recent acquisitions and NVIDIA collaboration show how its products are tied into next generation AI infrastructure. Earnings momentum, stronger margins, and forecasts for high returns on equity support that story. At the same time, a rich P/E and a balance sheet fully funded by external borrowing indicate that investors are paying up for this quality and taking on funding risk. For anyone tracking AI infrastructure, Vertiv presents a mix of fast growing, high quality fundamentals and valuation considerations that investors may want to assess when deciding where it belongs in a portfolio.

Vertiv’s AI build out story is accelerating, but most investors may be focusing on the headlines and not the underlying assumptions baked into today’s valuation. Put the pieces together with the analyst forecasts for Vertiv Holdings Co and see what might be hiding in plain sight.

UiPath (PATH)

Overview: UiPath is an automation software company that helps businesses use AI agents and software robots to handle repetitive processes, from back office workflows to testing and quality assurance, across sectors such as financial services, healthcare, manufacturing, retail and government.

Operations: UiPath generates about US$1.7b in revenue from Software & Programming, with around US$738.8m from the United States and further revenue across Europe, the Middle East and Africa and the rest of the Americas.

Market Cap: US$5.54b

UiPath gives investors exposure to automation and agentic AI at a time when many companies are trying to cut costs and speed up workflows. Its recent move into profitability with US$418.38m in quarterly revenue and US$22.53m in net income shows the business model can support earnings. The stock currently trades on a P/E below many software peers, and the company still has to manage headwinds from foreign exchange, deal timing and the shift toward SaaS that is weighing on near term growth. That mix of profitability, AI exposure and real transition risk is what makes UiPath worth a closer look for founder led opportunities.

UiPath’s shift to earnings and AI focus could be masking what really matters for long term holders. See how the DCF valuation analysis for UiPath frames that trade off and what the cash flows might be missing.

Tesla (TSLA)

Overview: Tesla is a US based company that designs, builds, sells and leases electric vehicles and also develops energy storage and solar products, supported by its own charging network, financing, insurance and software offerings such as self driving and vehicle infotainment. Beyond cars, Tesla provides home and grid scale batteries, solar panels and solar roofs, backed by installation, servicing and various financing options for residential, commercial and utility customers.

Market Cap: US$1.40t

Tesla attracts attention because it sits at the crossroads of electric vehicles, AI driven self driving software, humanoid robotics and large scale energy storage. However, today most of its cash flow is still tied to relatively low margin car sales. Earnings fell 35.3% last year and profit margins are a modest 3.7%. Analysts expect earnings growth above 20% a year and revenue growth ahead of the wider US market, which helps explain why the stock trades on a rich P/S multiple. At the same time, investors are weighing heavy AI and robotics capex, regulatory scrutiny around Autopilot and FSD, and ongoing litigation against the potential upside from robotaxis, Optimus and a growing Megapack backlog that could all materially reshape Tesla’s earnings mix over time.

Tesla’s earnings may look stalled, but the real story is how expectations for self driving, robotics and energy could reshape the whole equation. See how the analyst forecasts for Tesla reframes what the market might be missing

Hinge Health (HNGE)

Operations: Hinge Health generates about US$646.3m in revenue from Healthcare Software.

Market Cap: US$6.12b

Hinge Health is drawing attention because it combines fast growing healthcare software revenue with a large installed base of about 24.6 million contracted lives across more than 2,800 clients, including many Fortune 500 employers. Its AI tools and the Robin assistant are already supporting higher member volumes without rising care team costs. This ties directly to the margin story that bullish analysts and recent guidance upgrades are focused on. At the same time, the company is still unprofitable, carries high funding risk and pays rich executive compensation, so expectations are high. The key question for investors is whether its data advantage, new programs like HingeSelect and fresh federal and Medicare channels can outweigh those pressures and justify current pricing over time.

data.title: CSX (CSX) Stock Faces Questions As Q2 EPS Growth Lags Bullish Narratives
data.description:
CSX (CSX) has just posted Q2 2026 revenue of US$3,935 million with basic EPS of US$0.54, alongside trailing 12 month revenue of US$14.5 billion and EPS of US$1.73 that frame the latest quarter in a wider context. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$3,574 million and EPS of US$0.44 in Q2 2025 to US$3,935 million and EPS of US$0.54 in Q2 2026. Trailing 12 month revenue has ranged between US$14.1 billion and US$14.5 billion, and EPS between US$1.54 and US$1.73 over the past year. With net profit margins holding in the low 20s, this set of results gives investors a clean look at how CSX is converting its revenue base into consistent profitability.

See our full analysis for CSX.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how CSX's latest results line up with the most common narratives around its growth, profitability, and risk profile, and where those stories might need updating.

See what the community is saying about CSX

Advertisement

TTM revenue and margins hold steady around US$14.5b and 22.2%

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, CSX has reported about US$14.5b in revenue and US$3.2b in net income, which translates to a 22.2% net profit margin compared with 21.9% a year earlier.
  • Consensus narrative expects margin expansion over time, and the current 22.2% margin provides a concrete reference point against that view, as:
    • Analysts are assuming margins could move toward around 26.2% in a few years, so the present level shows some gap between today and that medium term target.
    • The modest change from 21.9% to 22.2% aligns with the idea of gradual, rather than rapid, efficiency gains that are still consistent with the consensus outlook.

CSX earnings growth at 3.9% trails forecast 8.9%

  • Over the last year, CSX's earnings grew 3.9% while the supplied data notes earnings are forecast to grow about 8.9% per year, highlighting a gap between recent performance and expectations.
  • Bulls point to volume opportunities and cost programs, and the current numbers partly support that optimism but also set some guardrails:
    • Bullish assumptions look for revenue growth of around 7% annually and a move in margins from roughly the low 20s toward the high 20s, whereas the latest trailing figures show revenue growth of 4.6% and margin at 22.2%.
    • This combination supports the bullish case that CSX is already profitable with room to improve, while reminding investors that the step up from 3.9% actual earnings growth to 8.9% forecast growth still needs to be earned through execution on projects like industrial development and intermodal expansion.

Bulls argue CSX's cost discipline and project pipeline could make the recent 3.9% earnings growth a starting point rather than the ceiling, and you can see how that argument stacks up in the 🐂 CSX Bull Case

Valuation wedged between P/E of 30.4x and DCF fair value of US$46.57

  • CSX trades on a P/E of 30.4x, slightly above the 30.1x peer average but below the broader US Transportation industry at 37.9x, while the current share price of US$52.81 sits above the DCF fair value of about US$46.57.
  • Critics highlight debt and pricing as key concerns, and the data offers a mixed read for that more cautious, bearish angle:
    • The high level of debt is flagged as a minor risk, which lines up with bears who worry that capital intensive projects and leverage could limit flexibility even if earnings grow.
    • At the same time, a P/E that is lower than the wider Transportation group but higher than peers, along with a price above DCF fair value, reinforces the bearish claim that investors should think carefully about how much of the 8.9% forecast earnings growth and margin improvement is already reflected in the current valuation.

Skeptics warn that paying above a DCF fair value of US$46.57 with a P/E of 30.4x only makes sense if CSX hits those mid to high single digit earnings growth targets, and you can see how that cautious view is built in the 🐻 CSX Bear Case

data.title: Core Lithium Stock And 2 Tariff Exempt Picks Worth Watching
data.description:
New U.S. tariffs of 10% and 12.5% on imports from major partners like the EU and China keep trade tensions in focus, even as many key products such as oil, gas and major food items remain exempt. For investors, that mix of pressure and protection can shift costs, pricing power and sentiment in uneven ways across the market. This article looks at three stocks whose businesses appear closely tied to these import exemptions and trade terms. It aims to help you think through where tariff exposure might be lower, where it might still matter and how that could influence each stock’s risk profile.

Advertisement

Core Lithium (ASX:CXO)

Overview: Core Lithium is an Australian resources company focused on developing lithium projects and other metal deposits in the Northern Territory and South Australia, targeting minerals such as gold, silver, uranium, lead, zinc, rare earths and base metals.

Market Cap: A$825.4m

Core Lithium sits at the intersection of two themes that matter for U.S. Import-Exempted Industries right now: critical minerals supply and tariff exemptions. As an Australian lithium developer with projects that can supply exempt critical minerals into the U.S., it operates under relatively stable trade terms while many competitors face higher import costs. The company carries meaningful risks, including current losses, past shareholder dilution and execution uncertainty around restarting operations and improving processing efficiency. It also holds cash with no debt, has drilled into additional lithium and gold potential, and plans to spin out non-core exploration into Axiant Resources, which could sharpen its focus. Investors considering this stock may wish to pay particular attention to these trade, financial and operational dynamics when conducting their own research.

Core Lithium’s stalled operations, clean balance sheet and tariff friendly positioning could be masking a sharper turning point than the market appreciates. It may be worth reading the 1 key reward and 2 important warning signs (2 are major!)

Avino Silver & Gold Mines (TSX:ASM)

Overview: Avino Silver & Gold Mines is a Vancouver based precious metals company that acquires, explores and advances silver, gold, copper and base metal projects in Mexico, anchored by its 100% owned Avino Mine complex in Durango.

Operations: Avino Silver & Gold Mines generates US$112.8m in revenue from metals and mining activities focused on gold and other precious metals, all originating from operations in Mexico.

Market Cap: CA$1.50b

Avino Silver & Gold Mines stands out in the U.S. Import Exempted Industries context because its silver and critical mineral output can move into the U.S. under tariff friendly terms, supporting pricing power when many peers face higher import costs. The business combines forecast double digit earnings and revenue growth with a 32.7% net margin, but the P/E of 26.1x, high reliance on external borrowing, dilution and recent insider selling mean investors are paying for that growth with added risk. Recent results show both strong year on year earnings and some pressure on production volumes, so anyone looking at the stock may want to understand how Avino plans to balance capital spending, debt and production scale under this new tariff regime.

Avino Silver & Gold Mines looks like growth at a price, with solid margins and tariff friendly access to the U.S. that many miss. Before you decide what the P/E of 26.1x is really signaling, read the 3 key rewards and 3 important warning signs (1 is major!)

TAT Technologies (TATT)

Overview: TAT Technologies provides maintenance, repair and overhaul services and manufactures heat transfer systems and other components for commercial and military aircraft, supporting airlines, cargo carriers and defense customers worldwide.

Operations: TAT Technologies generates most of its revenue from MRO Services for Aviation Components at US$83.8m, followed by MRO Services for Heat Transfer Components and OEM of Heat Transfer Solutions at US$43.1m, OEM of Heat Transfer Solutions and Aviation Accessories at US$42.1m, and Overhaul and Coating of Jet Engine Components at US$9.5m, with a small offset from inter company eliminations of US$1.4m.

Market Cap: US$538.2m

TAT Technologies sits in a favorable position under the new U.S. tariff regime, because aircraft parts are exempt and the company already generates substantial revenue from the United States. As a result, its MRO and heat transfer businesses avoid extra import costs that could affect less protected suppliers. A record backlog of about US$580m, fresh multi year MRO contracts and an exclusive global distribution deal for key Honeywell APU platforms provide revenue visibility and support an ongoing margin improvement story. At the same time, reliance on cyclical MRO demand, supply chain constraints and a balance sheet funded entirely with external borrowing mean the growth case involves meaningful risks, which is one reason some investors are watching TAT Technologies closely at present.

TAT Technologies looks like an overlooked aircraft supplier with a record US$580m backlog and fresh multi year deals that could reshape its earnings profile. Before assuming the story is straightforward, scan the analyst forecasts for TAT Technologies

data.title: Banner Corporation (NASDAQ:BANR) Just Reported Second-Quarter Earnings: Have Analysts Changed Their Mind On The Stock?
data.description:
Banner Corporation (NASDAQ:BANR) shareholders are probably feeling a little disappointed, since its shares fell 4.9% to US$68.85 in the week after its latest second-quarter results. It looks like the results were a bit of a negative overall. While revenues of US$175m were in line with analyst predictions, statutory earnings were less than expected, missing estimates by 2.4% to hit US$1.43 per share. The analysts typically update their forecasts at each earnings report, and we can judge from their estimates whether their view of the company has changed or if there are any new concerns to be aware of. We've gathered the most recent statutory forecasts to see whether the analysts have changed their earnings models, following these results.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Taking into account the latest results, the most recent consensus for Banner from four analysts is for revenues of US$734.0m in 2026. If met, it would imply a meaningful 9.3% increase on its revenue over the past 12 months. Statutory per-share earnings are expected to be US$6.04, roughly flat on the last 12 months. In the lead-up to this report, the analysts had been modelling revenues of US$722.7m and earnings per share (EPS) of US$6.17 in 2026. The analysts seem to have become a little more negative on the business after the latest results, given the minor downgrade to their earnings per share numbers for next year.

View our latest analysis for Banner

The consensus price target held steady at US$73.67, with the analysts seemingly voting that their lower forecast earnings are not expected to lead to a lower stock price in the foreseeable future. Fixating on a single price target can be unwise though, since the consensus target is effectively the average of analyst price targets. As a result, some investors like to look at the range of estimates to see if there are any diverging opinions on the company's valuation. There are some variant perceptions on Banner, with the most bullish analyst valuing it at US$77.00 and the most bearish at US$70.00 per share. The narrow spread of estimates could suggest that the business' future is relatively easy to value, or thatthe analysts have a strong view on its prospects.

Of course, another way to look at these forecasts is to place them into context against the industry itself. The analysts are definitely expecting Banner's growth to accelerate, with the forecast 19% annualised growth to the end of 2026 ranking favourably alongside historical growth of 1.1% per annum over the past five years. By contrast, our data suggests that other companies (with analyst coverage) in a similar industry are forecast to grow their revenue at 7.6% per year. It seems obvious that, while the growth outlook is brighter than the recent past, the analysts also expect Banner to grow faster than the wider industry.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The biggest concern is that the analysts reduced their earnings per share estimates, suggesting business headwinds could lay ahead for Banner. Fortunately, they also reconfirmed their revenue numbers, suggesting that it's tracking in line with expectations. data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see The Brink's Company (NYSE:BCO) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next 2 days. The ex-dividend date is one business day before the record date, which is the cut-off date for shareholders to be present on the company's books to be eligible for a dividend payment. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. Meaning, you will need to purchase Brink's' shares before the 27th of July to receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 1st of September.

The company's upcoming dividend is US$0.255 a share, following on from the last 12 months, when the company distributed a total of US$1.02 per share to shareholders. Last year's total dividend payments show that Brink's has a trailing yield of 0.8% on the current share price of US$121.09. We love seeing companies pay a dividend, but it's also important to be sure that laying the golden eggs isn't going to kill our golden goose! So we need to investigate whether Brink's can afford its dividend, and if the dividend could grow.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Dividends are usually paid out of company profits, so if a company pays out more than it earned then its dividend is usually at greater risk of being cut. Brink's paid out just 24% of its profit last year, which we think is conservatively low and leaves plenty of margin for unexpected circumstances. Yet cash flow is typically more important than profit for assessing dividend sustainability, so we should always check if the company generated enough cash to afford its dividend. Luckily it paid out just 7.8% of its free cash flow last year.

It's positive to see that Brink's's dividend is covered by both profits and cash flow, since this is generally a sign that the dividend is sustainable, and a lower payout ratio usually suggests a greater margin of safety before the dividend gets cut.
